title: Huevos rancheros
subtitle: Fried eggs on corn tortillas in a spiced tomato salsa
excerpt: The Mexican ranch breakfast adopted into the American morning repertoire: corn tortillas warmed in oil, fried eggs placed directly on top, and a hot, garlicky tomato and chilli salsa spooned over everything. Black beans and soured cream alongside. Ready in twenty minutes.
sourceNotes: Huevos rancheros (ranch eggs) is a traditional Mexican breakfast dish, documented in Mexican cooking throughout the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. It entered the broader American culinary mainstream through the Tex-Mex tradition of the American Southwest, particularly Texas and New Mexico, where Mexican and American cooking traditions have intersected since the nineteenth century. USDA agricultural extension materials from the southwestern United States from the early twentieth century reference similar egg and tortilla preparations.

content.text: The dish has three layers. A corn tortilla, crisped briefly in oil so it does not go limp. A fried egg with a runny yolk on top. And a ranchero salsa of tinned tomatoes, garlic, chilli, and cumin. The salsa is sharp, a little spicy, and loose enough to soak into the edges of the tortilla. Fry the eggs sunny-side up or over-easy. The yolk breaks when you cut into it and mixes with the salsa.

Make the salsa first so it has time to reduce slightly while you fry the tortillas and eggs. A tin of good tomatoes, blended briefly and seasoned with garlic, jalapeño, cumin, and coriander, produces a sauce with enough character to carry the dish. Serve with black beans from a tin warmed in the salsa, and a spoonful of soured cream.

Warm a little olive oil in a saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{jalapeno}} jalapeño and cook for 1 to 2 minutes until fragrant. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, the {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, and the {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar. Simmer for 8 to 10 minutes until slightly thickened. Season with salt. If you prefer a smoother sauce, blend briefly with a stick blender. Add the drained black beans in the last 2 minutes if using and stir to warm through.
Crisp the tortillas and fry the eggs

In a separate frying pan, warm a thin film of oil over medium-high heat. Add the {{corn-tortilla}} corn tortillas one at a time and fry for 30 to 45 seconds per side until they blister slightly and firm up. Keep warm. In the same pan, reduce the heat to medium and fry the {{eggs}} eggs sunny-side up, basting occasionally with the hot oil, for 2 to 3 minutes until the whites are just set and the yolks are still runny.

Place two crisped tortillas per person on a warm plate, slightly overlapping. Spoon a generous amount of salsa (with beans if using) over the tortillas. Place the fried eggs on top. Add a spoonful of soured cream alongside. Scatter coriander over if using.

items.fix: Fry the tortillas briefly until blistered; assemble at the last moment and serve immediately
items.cause: Tortillas not crisped before assembling, or assembled too far in advance
items.symptom: Tortillas go limp under the salsa

items.fix: Add a pinch of sugar to balance; taste before serving and adjust
items.cause: Too much vinegar or tomatoes that are particularly acidic

items.fix: Fry on medium heat and remove the pan from heat while the yolk is still visibly liquid; residual heat will set the white without cooking the yolk through
items.cause: Fried too long or heat too high

items.fix: Include the seeds for more heat, or add a pinch of chilli flakes to the salsa
items.cause: One jalapeño is mild when seeds are removed

content.text: Huevos rancheros is the traditional breakfast of Mexican ranch workers. They ate it before a long day in the fields. It is filling and fast. It uses ingredients that were always to hand. The dish crossed into American cooking through Tex-Mex kitchens in the Southwest. It appears on diner and brunch menus in Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, and California. From the 1970s on, US interest in Mexican food spread the dish across the country. It is a morning dish that works at any hour.
